# How to Get Listed on the Army UAS Marketplace

> **Last updated:** April 2026  
> **Audience:** Manufacturers, integrators, and vendors seeking to sell UAS systems or related technology to U.S. Army units, DoD agencies, and allied nations

---

## What Is the Army UAS Marketplace?

Launched on March 24, 2026, the U.S. Army's Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS) Marketplace is a digital storefront — built with AWS and the Army Enterprise Cloud Management Agency — where military units, government partners, and allied nations can browse, compare, and procure vetted drone systems. The Army's stated goal is to make buying drones as straightforward as placing an Amazon order.

As of launch, 30 systems are listed (all Group 1 and 2 platforms drawn from existing PMO UAS programs). Group 3 drones are expected to be added by summer 2026. Future expansion will include Blue UAS List systems and Drone Dominance Gauntlet competition winners.

**Solicitation number:** `W58RGZ-26-S-C001`  
**Contracting office:** Army Contracting Command – Redstone (ACC-RSA)  
**Program office contact:** PM UAS, Capability Program Executive Office for Aviation  

---

## Prerequisites Before You Apply

Before you submit anything, make sure you have these in place. Missing any of them will block an award.

**1. Unique Entity Identifier (UEI)**  
You need a UEI from SAM.gov. This replaced the old DUNS number system. If you don't have one, register at sam.gov — it's free but can take 7–10 business days to process.

**2. Active SAM.gov Registration**  
Your company must be registered and active in the System for Award Management (SAM.gov). SAM registration must be renewed annually. Awards cannot be issued to entities with lapsed registration.

**3. WAWF Access**  
Wide Area Workflow (WAWF) is the DoD's invoicing and receiving system. You'll need an account when it's time to get paid. Set this up early — it's a separate system from SAM.

**4. NDAA §848 Compliance**  
The marketplace is for vetted platforms. If your UAS contains components from prohibited foreign manufacturers (primarily Chinese-origin flight controllers, cameras, or comms), you will not pass vetting. Review the NDAA §848 covered list before applying.

**5. Blue UAS Listing (strongly recommended, not yet mandatory)**  
The marketplace is actively adding Blue UAS List systems. Being on the Blue UAS List significantly speeds up vetting. If you're not listed yet, the DIU Blue UAS pathway is a parallel track worth pursuing — see the [Blue UAS Compliance Guide](/compliance/) for that process.

---

## The Two Submission Pathways

The CSO operates two submission methods. Choose based on your situation.

### Pathway 1 — Innovative Warfighter Solutions (IWS)

This is the **open-ended, submit-anytime** pathway. You respond to one or more of the Army's general Areas of Interest (AOI) without waiting for a specific government request.

**Use this if:**
- You have a product ready or near-ready and want to get in front of the Army
- You're not responding to a specific government-issued requirement
- You want to establish a relationship and get on the Army's radar

**Areas of Interest currently include:**
- Air vehicles and payloads (Group 1–3 UAS)
- Intelligence, electronic warfare, and sensors
- Tactical communications
- Autonomous behaviors and C2 software
- Launched Effects (one-way attack drones)
- Soldier equipment
- Simulation and training tools
- Supply chain, logistics, and additive manufacturing
- Production of UAS at scale
- Ancillary items

**What to submit:**  
A solution brief. This is not a full proposal — it's a concise technical overview of your capability, how it maps to an AOI, your company's production capacity, and your compliance posture (NDAA, ITAR, etc.). If your brief is compelling, the Army will follow up with an invitation for a live demo, pitch, or full commercial solution proposal.

---

### Pathway 2 — Call for Solutions (C4S)

This is the **specific-requirement, deadline-driven** pathway. The Army posts a specific need on SAM.gov as an amendment to the CSO, and vendors respond within a defined window.

**Use this if:**
- The Army has posted a specific requirement that matches your product
- You're responding to a Gauntlet competition or Blue UAS expansion call
- You want a faster, more direct path to a contract for a defined capability

**What to submit:**  
A full commercial solution proposal in response to the government's stated requirements. These submissions have specific response deadlines and technical evaluation criteria published in each C4S amendment on SAM.gov.

---

## Step-by-Step: How to Submit

### Step 1 — Find the active solicitation on SAM.gov

Go to [sam.gov/opportunities](https://sam.gov/opportunities) and search:

```
W58RGZ-26-S-C001
```

Download the full CSO document. Read the entire thing — it contains the submission format requirements, evaluation criteria, and contact information. The CSO remains open indefinitely unless formally canceled.

### Step 2 — Choose your pathway

IWS = you have something and want to pitch it.  
C4S = the Army posted a specific need you can answer.

For most new vendors entering the marketplace, IWS is the starting point.

### Step 3 — Prepare your solution brief (IWS) or proposal (C4S)

For IWS solution briefs, cover:

- **Executive Summary** — what the system is, what problem it solves, which AOI it addresses
- **Technical Description** — specs, performance data, Group classification, payload options
- **Compliance Status** — NDAA §848, ITAR, export controls, Blue UAS listing status
- **Production Capacity** — your current and scalable production numbers; the Army specifically cares about industrial base capacity
- **Company Background** — SAM registration, UEI, key personnel, past performance with government or defense customers if applicable
- **Pricing** (general range) — the CSO is a free-market model; competitive pricing matters

Keep it tight. This is an initial filter, not a contract. 5–15 pages is typically appropriate for a solution brief.

### Step 4 — Contact the contracting officers

Before submitting, reach out to the POCs listed in the CSO:

- **Miguel Harris** — miguel.c.harris.civ@army.mil  
- **Latoya Pryor** — latoya.s.pryor.civ@army.mil

Confirm the current submission format and whether there are any active C4S amendments that match your capability. Contracting officers can't give you preferential treatment, but they can confirm you're submitting correctly.

### Step 5 — Submit through SAM.gov

All submissions go through the SAM.gov opportunity posting for `W58RGZ-26-S-C001`. Do not email submissions directly to the contracting officers unless the solicitation explicitly instructs otherwise.

### Step 6 — Army vetting and testing

If your brief passes initial review, you'll be invited to a demonstration or pitch. The Army will test what you have. Once approved, your system gets listed on the marketplace and units can start placing orders.

Per Col. Medaglia: *"You tell us when you're ready. We're going to tell you what the soldiers want... when you're ready, we're going to test what you have, and we're going to provide it on the marketplace."*

---

## What Gets You Approved Faster

Based on the CSO structure and public statements from PM UAS leadership:

**Already on the Blue UAS List** — Systems from the Blue List are actively being onboarded. This is the fastest existing path to the marketplace.

**Gauntlet competition winner** — Drone Dominance Gauntlet winners (Phase 1 covers 25 vendors competing for $150M in delivery orders) will be added to the marketplace.

**Demonstrated production capacity** — The Army has explicitly called out industrial base scaling as a priority. Vendors who can show they can produce at volume (not just prototypes) get prioritized.

**NDAA-clean supply chain** — Any foreign-component ambiguity slows vetting. Document your supply chain proactively.

**Existing Army relationship** — Systems already under a PMO UAS program of record were the first 30 entries. If you have an existing contract or OTA with Army, reference it.

---

## Contract Types Available

Awards under this CSO can be structured as:

- **FAR contracts** — standard Federal Acquisition Regulation contracts (FFP, IDIQ, etc.)
- **Other Transaction Agreements (OTAs)** — non-FAR agreements under 10 U.S.C. 4022; faster to award, more flexible, commonly used for prototype and production at scale

OTAs awarded for prototyping can lead directly to follow-on production contracts without additional competition under 10 U.S.C. 4022(f) — this is a significant advantage for vendors who get in early.

---

## Upcoming Events

- **April 14, 2026** — Army UAS Marketplace Showcase, prior to the Army Aviation Association of America (AAAA) Warfighting Summit. Attendance is high-value for making direct contact with PM UAS leadership.
- **Ongoing** — Additional C4S amendments to the CSO are expected to be posted on SAM.gov as the Army defines more specific capability needs.

| Contracting Office | Army Contracting Command – Redstone |
| Primary POC | Miguel Harris, miguel.c.harris.civ@army.mil |
| Secondary POC | Latoya Pryor, latoya.s.pryor.civ@army.mil |
| Open until | Indefinitely (until Army cancels) |
| Submission pathways | IWS (open-ended) / C4S (specific calls) |
| Current platform count | 30 systems (Group 1–2 at launch) |
| Award types | FAR contracts or OTAs |
| SAM registration | Required before award |
